Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

---

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

Budget used so far: $123.07 / $1000.00

  `\oddsidemargin`, `\evensidemargin`, `\topmargin`, etc.
- Do not change line spacing: no `setspace`, `\linespread`,

- Do not change font size inside the document: no `\small`,

### Workspace files ###

- research_notes.tex: read from `/mnt/data/file-TZsVZhWmFEQEQnCDkytaeL-research_notes.tex` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/research_notes.tex`.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on the revised draft

### Verdict

The revision fixes one major defect from the previous version: it no longer uses the false claim that a non-normal state taking value \(1\) on several noncommuting projections must be supported on their lattice meet. The new “palindromic product” idea is a reasonable way to avoid that specific error.

However, `answer.tex` is still **not answer-ready**. The proof now depends on a new graph-product boundary-corner intersection lemma whose key normal-bidual/support step is not proved and is not a stated consequence of the cited references. There is also a serious unresolved issue in the finite-boundary criterion: the proof identifies the compact support corner \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) with \(\mathcal K(L^2N)^{**}=B(L^2N)\), but this is not justified in DKEP’s normal-bidual setup and is likely not literally correct as stated.

### LaTeX contract

I recreated the revised `answer.tex` and ran `pdflatex` twice. It compiled successfully to a 4-page PDF. The document uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses `fullpage`, and I found no forbidden margin, line-spacing, or in-document font-size changes. The LaTeX contract appears satisfied.

### Literature cross-check

DKEP indeed define \(S_X(M)\) and prove the generator-checking lemma used for membership in \(S_X(M)\); their Lemma 6.1 says one may check the commutator condition on an ultraweakly dense generating subalgebra. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Example 5.11 defines coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives the normal-bidual characterization of relative proper proximality under the relevant separability and center-ergodicity hypotheses. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

CDHJKN do provide the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules cited in the proof. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) They also give the factoriality criterion used in the last section. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Their Main Theorem 0.1 gives the relative amenability criterion used for nonamenability. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The problem is not that these references are irrelevant; rather, the draft uses them to infer additional support-projection and normal-bidual statements that are not stated in those references and are not proved in the draft.

## Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The author explicitly adopts the standard graph-product interpretation of irreducibility as nontrivial join-irreducibility, equivalently connectedness of the complement graph. That resolves the ambiguity in the problem statement.

### 2. “Two boundary facts”: setup

The normal-bidual notation is broadly consistent with DKEP Lemma 8.5. For a factor and the trivial action, the center-ergodicity hypothesis in Lemma 8.5 is automatic, so using the normal-bidual no-central-state criterion is reasonable. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the draft silently assumes several structural facts about the support projections \(q_X\) and corners \(q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X\) that are not proved. This becomes serious in the two subsequent lemmas.

### 3. Lemma: intersection of graph-product boundary corners

This is the main unresolved gap.

The lemma asserts
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\tag{1}
\]
The proof tries to derive this from coefficient boundary pieces and CDHJKN fusion rules. But the crucial implication
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
\subset q_{\mathcal L}\mathcal A_Mq_{\mathcal L}
\tag{3}
\]
is not established.

DKEP Example 5.11 defines the coefficient boundary piece \(X_{\mathcal H}\) of a correspondence \(\mathcal H\), but it does **not** state the normal-support calculus needed here. In particular, the proof needs a precise theorem of the following kind:

> If \(X_{\mathcal H}\) and \(X_{\mathcal K}\) are coefficient boundary pieces, then sandwiching the normal support corner of \(X_{\mathcal H}\) by the support of \(X_{\mathcal K}\) is dominated by the coefficient support of an appropriate Connes fusion correspondence.

No such theorem is cited or proved.

There is also a coefficient-order problem. DKEP’s coefficient boundary is generated by operators of the form \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\). Under Connes fusion, DKEP’s formula is \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) From this formula, coefficients of a triple fusion are not automatically arbitrary products of a \(W\)-coefficient, a \(U\)-coefficient, and a \(W\)-coefficient in the order needed to identify \(q_W(q_U\mathcal A q_U)q_W\). The sentence

> “translating the three tensor factors gives the general generators”

is therefore insufficient and potentially misleading.

Even after applying CDHJKN’s fusion rules, one still needs to prove that coefficient boundary pieces behave correctly under possibly infinite direct sums and multiplicities. CDHJKN prove Hilbert-bimodule decompositions; they do not identify DKEP normal-bidual support projections or prove containment of coefficient boundary pieces after passing to the DKEP support corners. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

Thus Lemma 1 remains an essential unproved lemma. Since the final palindromic compact-corner inclusion depends entirely on it, this is fatal.

### 4. Lemma: finite palindromic boundary criterion

The revision successfully fixes the previous invalid meet-of-projections argument. The palindromic product
\[
R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_m
\]
is a positive contraction, and if a state \(\omega\) satisfies \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for every \(i\), then its GNS cyclic vector is fixed by each \(q_i\), hence by \(R\). This part is sound.

The compression argument also looks essentially correct: if \(\omega(q_i^\perp)>0\), compression by \(q_i^\perp\) gives an \(N\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\), and normality on \(N\) follows by domination by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\).

However, the final step is not justified as written:

> “The compact corner \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) is canonically \(\mathcal K(L^2N)^{**}=B(L^2N)\).”

In DKEP’s setup, \(q_X\) is the support of the normal corner associated with \(\mathbb K_X(M)\), not simply the bidual of the original hereditary algebra \(X\). For \(X=\mathcal K(L^2M)\), DKEP’s associated relatively compact spaces are not just the literal compact operators; DKEP explicitly notes that \(K^{\infty,1}(M)\) can be much larger than \(\mathcal K(L^2M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) Therefore the identification
\[
q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\cong \mathcal K(L^2N)^{**}=B(L^2N)
\]
needs a proof or a precise citation. It is not a formal consequence of the definitions as presented.

The intended conclusion may be salvageable by using DKEP’s natural u.c.p. map from \(B(L^2N)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner, but that map is not described in `answer.tex`. As written, the hypertrace step is unsupported.

### 5. Proposition: relative Bass–Serre paradox

This section is improved compared to the earlier draft. The commutator formulas
\[
[p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0
\]
for \(x\in P\ominus B\), and the analogous formulas for \(x\in Q\ominus B\), are plausible and are the right kind of calculation. DKEP Lemma 6.1 is indeed the relevant tool for reducing membership in \(S_{X_B}(N)\) to generators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Still, the proof remains terse. The formulas are asserted by “inspection of reduced words” rather than proved. For a final rigorous proof, one should explicitly verify the action on the \(B\)-summand, one-letter words, and longer reduced words, including the collapse-to-\(B\) case. This is a lesser issue than the boundary-corner lemma, because the formulas are likely correct, but the current exposition is still somewhat handwavy.

The paradoxical inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
are plausible and the central-state contradiction is standard once \(p,q,e_B\in S_{X_B}(N)\). This section is probably repairable with a fuller reduced-word calculation.

### 6. Application to graph products

The graph-product decomposition
\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\operatorname{Lk}(v)}}
(M_{\operatorname{Lk}(v)}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]
is standard. The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) in the two complement-neighbor cases appears correct.

The factoriality argument using CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 is sound: connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices rules out both universal vertices and isolated exceptional complement edges. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The nonamenability argument using CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.1 is also essentially sound: if \(M\) were amenable, then applying the relative amenability criterion with \(V_1=V\), \(V_2=\varnothing\) would force every nonedge to be an exceptional pair adjacent to all remaining vertices, which would make each complement edge an isolated component, impossible in a connected complement graph with at least three vertices. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But the final passage from relative proper proximality with respect to the link boundaries to ordinary proper proximality depends on Lemma 1 and Lemma 2. Since those remain insufficiently justified, the final theorem is not proved.

## Previous concerns: addressed or remaining

- **Previous false meet/state argument:** addressed by the palindromic-product criterion.
- **Previous unsupported fusion-meet identity:** not fully addressed. It has been replaced by a weaker intersection lemma, but that lemma still requires an unproved DKEP normal-support/fusion calculus.
- **Previous handwavy \(p,q\in S_{X_B}\):** partially addressed by explicit formulas, though the reduced-word verification is still abbreviated.
- **New issue introduced/emphasized:** the finite-boundary criterion now relies on the unproved identification \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\cong B(L^2N)\), which is not justified in DKEP’s normal-bidual framework.

### Final assessment

The revised draft is closer than the previous one, but it still contains essential unproved lemmas in the boundary-support part of the argument. The proof is therefore not complete.

### Workflow compile/format feedback ###
No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

### Advisory Council replies (if you requested any last turn) ###
### Council member: gpt-55-pro (models/openai/gpt-55-pro) ###
I would not leave this lemma as written. The inclusion is very plausible, but the step

\[
\text{products of coefficients } \Longrightarrow
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{\mathcal L}\mathcal A_Mq_{\mathcal L}
\]

is exactly where noncommutativity of support projections matters. It needs an abstract “support-sandwich” lemma; otherwise it reads like an implicit meet/commutation argument.

I would replace the proof by first proving a C\(^*\)-level containment. Let  
\(\mathfrak K_U=(\mathbb K_{X_{M_U}}(M)^\sharp_J)\), so that
\(\mathfrak K_U^{**}=q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U\). Prove:

\[
\overline{\operatorname{span}}\,\mathfrak K_W\mathfrak K_U\mathfrak K_W
\subset \mathfrak K_{U\cap W}. \tag{*}
\]

This is the real graph-product/fusion statement. DKEP’s coefficient formula gives

\[
\mathfrak K_W\mathfrak K_U\mathfrak K_W
\subset \mathfrak K_{\mathcal L},
\qquad
\mathcal L=\mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_U\otimes_M\mathcal H_W,
\]

after taking the hereditary/norm-closed coefficient boundary. Then CDHJKN fusion gives \(\mathcal L\) as a direct sum of \(\mathcal H_Z=L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M\) with \(Z\subset U\cap W\). To conclude \(\mathfrak K_{\mathcal L}\subset \mathfrak K_{U\cap W}\), explicitly cite/prove the monotonicity lemma:

> If \(B\subset C\subset M\) are expected subalgebras, then \(X_B\subset X_C\).

For Jones pieces this follows from \(e_B\le e_C\) and hereditary closure of the boundary piece generated by \(e_C\). Thus \(Z\subset U\cap W\) implies \(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_{U\cap W}}\). Direct sums of correspondences give closed sums of coefficient boundaries, so \(\mathfrak K_{\mathcal L}\subset\mathfrak K_{U\cap W}\). This proves \((*)\).

Then insert the missing general lemma:

> **Support-sandwich lemma.** Let \(A\) be the ambient C\(^*\)-algebra and let \(B,C,D\subset A\) be hereditary C\(^*\)-subalgebras with support projections \(p_B,p_C,p_D\in A^{**}\). If  
> \[
> \overline{\operatorname{span}}\, C B C\subset D,
> \]
> then
> \[
> p_C(p_BA^{**}p_B)p_C\subset p_DA^{**}p_D.
> \]

Proof: for \(x\in p_BA^{**}p_B=B^{**}\), approximate \(x\) ultraweakly by bounded \(b_i\in B\). For \(c,d\in C\), \(cb_id\in CBC\subset D\), hence \(cxd\in D^{**}\). Let \(c,d\) run through an approximate unit of \(C\); the ultraweak limit is \(p_Cxp_C\in D^{**}\).

Applying this with \(B=\mathfrak K_U\), \(C=\mathfrak K_W\), \(D=\mathfrak K_{U\cap W}\) gives precisely

\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]

So: the inclusion can be made rigorous, but as written it skips the crucial support-sandwich passage. Add that lemma and the Jones-piece monotonicity statement; then the palindromic finite-boundary upgrade avoids any assumption that the \(q_U\)’s commute.

### Council member: opus_47_max (models/anthropic/opus_47_max) ###
The strategy is right but two steps are compressed in a way I think a careful reader (or referee) will flag. Here is how I would re-organize, with the specific sub-lemmas to insert.

**(1) Algebraic intermediate before any bidual.** Prove first, at the $C^*$-level,
$$\mathbb{K}_{X_W}(M)\cdot \mathbb{K}_{X_U}(M)\cdot \mathbb{K}_{X_W}(M)\;\subset\; \mathbb{K}_{X_{\mathcal L}}(M).\qquad(*)$$
DKEP's $T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ gives this on triples of coefficient generators; the $M$-bimodule closure on the LHS absorbs into translates of elementary tensors on the RHS via DKEP's translation rules $aT_\xi b=T_{a\xi b}$. This is genuinely a separate statement from Example 5.11 (which is about a single $\mathcal H_U$) and is worth writing once, even briefly.

**(2) Bidual upgrade.** This is where "passing to hereditary closures and then to the normal bidual" hides an actual approximate-unit argument. Pick a positive contractive approximate unit $(e_\alpha)$ in $\mathbb K_{X_W}(M)$; then $e_\alpha\to q_W$ weak-* in $\mathcal A_M$. For $T\in q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U$, weak-* approximate $T=\lim_\beta S_\beta$ with $S_\beta\in\mathbb K_{X_U}(M)$. Multiplication is *separately* weak-* continuous, so by iterated (not joint) limits
$$q_WTq_W=\lim_\beta\lim_{\alpha,\gamma}\,e_\alpha S_\beta e_\gamma,$$
with each $e_\alpha S_\beta e_\gamma\in \mathbb K_{X_W}\mathbb K_{X_U}\mathbb K_{X_W}\subset \mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal L}}(M)$ by $(*)$. The triple limit therefore lies in $\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal L}}(M)^{**}=q_{\mathcal L}\mathcal A_Mq_{\mathcal L}$. Note that nothing in this step uses $[q_W,q_U]=0$ — which is precisely why the lemma is phrased as a corner inclusion rather than $q_W\wedge q_U=q_{U\cap W}$. I would move the caution from your research notes about non-commuting supports into the body of the proof, because it is the reason for this organization.

**(3) The $Z\subset U\cap W$ inclusion.** This is the step I would most encourage you to expand, because it is doing all the graph-product-specific work and the citation is bearing a lot of weight. One self-contained paragraph: in CDHJKN's reduced-word picture, a vector in the centered part of $L^2M\otimes_{M_W}L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\otimes_{M_W}L^2M$ is (after the standard simplifications) a reduced word whose outer two ends are centered modulo $M_W$ and whose middle tube is centered modulo $M_U$. The simultaneous $M$-stabilizer of such a word is the intersection of the link conditions imposed by every letter type appearing, and this intersection sits inside $U\cap W$ by the graph-product centralizer calculus. Then $X_{\mathcal L}\subset X_{M_{U\cap W}}$ and so $q_{\mathcal L}\le q_{U\cap W}$.

**Sanity checks** that will catch most slip-ups here: (i) for $U\subset W$, $(*)$ should give $X_{\mathcal L}\subset X_U$, matching $U\cap W=U$; (ii) for your actual application $\bigcap L_{v_i}=\varnothing$, the conclusion $q_{\mathcal L}\le q_\varnothing=q_0$ is exactly what Lemma 2 needs — worth verifying at the level of the empty-word sector.

With (1)–(3) written out, I think the lemma is rigorous as stated. The bilinear weak-* continuity in (2) is the most subtle ingredient and I would not skip it; the $Z\subset U\cap W$ paragraph in (3) is where I would invest the most additional words.

### Council member: gemini-31-pro (models/gemini/gemini-31-pro) ###
The overarching strategy you have proposed—bypassing the need for the projections to commute by using a palindromic product and checking the algebraic fusion rules—is highly elegant and structurally correct. 

To answer your question directly: your intuition is perfectly on target, but the transition labeled "Passing to hereditary closures and then to the normal bidual" currently skips a couple of functional analytic steps. Because the weak-* product of weakly dense sets is not necessarily weakly dense, you cannot map the inclusion directly to the corners $q_U \mathcal{A}_M q_U$. 

Fortunately, the workaround is purely algebraic and very clean. Here is how you can rewrite the middle of the proof of Lemma 1 to make it completely rigorous.

### 1. Upgrade from sets to C*-algebras
First, clarify that the coefficient boundaries are not just Banach spaces, but C*-algebras. Because $X_W$ is generated by $M$-translates of $e_{M_W}$, it is the norm closure of the dense *-algebra $X_W^0 = M e_{M_W} M$.
Your observation about the coefficients of $\mathcal{L}$ tells us exactly that the dense algebraic span of the coefficients of $\mathcal{L}$ is $X_W^0 X_U^0 X_W^0$. Taking norm closures immediately yields the C*-algebra inclusion:
\[
    X_W X_U X_W \subset X_{\mathcal{L}}.
\]

### 2. Passing to the bidual via projection inequalities
Because $X_W, X_U,$ and $X_{\mathcal{L}}$ are C*-algebras, you can pass to their biduals. Multiplication in a von Neumann algebra is separately weak-* continuous, meaning $a \mapsto x a y$ is continuous. This guarantees that the bidual von Neumann algebras satisfy:
\[
    X_W^{**} X_U^{**} X_W^{**} \subset X_{\mathcal{L}}^{**}.
\]
The support projections $q_W, q_U, q_{\mathcal{L}}$ in $\mathcal{A}_M$ are exactly the identity elements of these respective bidual algebras. Evaluating the above inclusion at the identities gives the projection inequality:
\[
    q_W q_U q_W \le q_{\mathcal{L}}.
\]

### 3. Recovering the corner inclusion
With the operator inequality in hand, the corner inclusion follows by standard von Neumann algebra mechanics. Since $q_W q_U q_W \le q_{\mathcal{L}}$, sandwiching by $q_{\mathcal{L}}^\perp$ yields:
\[
    q_{\mathcal{L}}^\perp q_W q_U q_W q_{\mathcal{L}}^\perp = 0 \implies q_{\mathcal{L}}^\perp q_W q_U = 0.
\]
Thus $q_W q_U = q_{\mathcal{L}} q_W q_U$. For any $T \in \mathcal{A}_M$, we then have:
\[
    q_W q_U T q_U q_W = (q_{\mathcal{L}} q_W q_U) T (q_U q_W q_{\mathcal{L}}) \in q_{\mathcal{L}} \mathcal{A}_M q_{\mathcal{L}}.
\]
This proves (3) rigorously without ever needing $q_W$ and $q_U$ to commute or worrying about weak density of products.

We verify Proposition \ref{prop:BS}.  Let
$C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}$.  Since $\Gamma^c$ is connected, $C(v)$
is nonempty.  If $C(v)$ contains distinct $r,t$, choose trace-zero unitaries
$u_r\in M_r$, $u_t\in M_t$ and set $w_1=u_r$, $w_2=u_t$.  The expectation onto
$M_{L_v}$ kills $w_1,w_2,w_1^*w_2$ because each word contains a centered letter
from a vertex outside $L_v$.

If $C(v)=\{r\}$, connectedness of $\Gamma^c$ and $|V|\ge3$ give
$s\ne v,r$ with $r\not\sim_\Gamma s$; then $s\in L_v$.  Choose trace-zero
unitaries $u_r\in M_r$, $u_s\in M_s$ and put $w_1=u_r$, $w_2=u_su_r$.  Again
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=E_{M_{L_v}}(u_r^*u_su_r)=0 .
\]
The given trace-zero unitary in $M_v$ supplies the unitary $a$ in the second
free-product leg of (7).  Hence $M$ is properly proximal relative to
$X_{M_{L_v}}$ for every $v\in V$.

Enumerate $V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}$.  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
$\cap_i L_{v_i}=\varnothing$.  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} gives
\[
 q_{L_{v_m}}\cdots q_{L_{v_1}}q_{L_{v_2}}\cdots q_{L_{v_m}}
 \in q_\varnothing\cA_Mq_\varnothing .
\]
Here $M_\varnothing=\C$, and $X_{M_\varnothing}$ is the compact boundary piece,
because $e_{\C}$ is rank one and its left and right $M$-translates generate the
finite-rank operators.

It remains only to check the hypotheses of Lemma \ref{lem:finite}.  The algebra
$M$ is separable.  By \cite[Main Theorem 0.5]{CDHJKN}, factoriality can fail
only through a universal vertex, or through an exceptional non-edge pair
adjacent to every other vertex with both vertex algebras two-dimensional.
These would give respectively an isolated vertex or an isolated edge in
$\Gamma^c$, impossible because $\Gamma^c$ is connected and has at least three
vertices.  Hence $M$ is a factor.

Also $M$ is non-amenable.  If it were amenable, then CDHJKN's relative
amenability criterion \cite[Main Theorem 0.1]{CDHJKN}, applied with
$V_1=V$ and $V_2=\varnothing$, would force every non-edge of $\Gamma$ to be an
exceptional pair adjacent to all remaining vertices.  In the complement graph,
every edge would then be an isolated component of size two, again impossible
for a connected graph with at least three vertices.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}
therefore applies and proves that $M$ is properly proximal.

\begin{thebibliography}{9}

\bibitem{DKEP}
C.~Ding, S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and J.~Peterson,
\emph{Properly proximal von Neumann algebras},
Duke Math. J. \textbf{172} (2023), 2821--2894.

\bibitem{CDHJKN}
I.~Charlesworth, R.~de Santiago, B.~Hayes, D.~Jekel,
S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and B.~Nelson,
\emph{On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras},
Publ. Res. Inst. Math. Sci. \textbf{61} (2025), 713--762.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}
"""

print(len(answer), answer.count('\n'))
